standard: OCP Microscaling (MX) spec v1.0 block-scale format
layout: 8-bit unsigned exponent, value = 2^(byte-127), byte=0x00 zero, byte=0xFF NaN
rounding_mode: OCP MX v1.0: floor(log2(|x|)) for scale extraction (ml_dtypes ground truth)
use_case: block scales in MXFP4, MXFP6, MXFP8 (OCP MX spec)

vectors.name: one
vectors.spec_reference: OCP MX v1.0 + ml_dtypes 0.5.4 ground truth

vectors.name: two
vectors.spec_reference: OCP MX v1.0 + ml_dtypes 0.5.4 ground truth

vectors.name: half
vectors.spec_reference: OCP MX v1.0 + ml_dtypes 0.5.4 ground truth

vectors.name: max_finite
vectors.spec_reference: OCP MX v1.0 + ml_dtypes 0.5.4 ground truth

vectors.name: min_subnormal_boundary
vectors.spec_reference: OCP MX v1.0 + ml_dtypes 0.5.4 ground truth

vectors.name: anchor_three_rounded
vectors.notes: 3.0 not a power-of-2; rounds to nearest log2 -> byte=128 (2^1=2) or byte=129 (2^2=4), RTNE picks even k -> byte=128
vectors.spec_reference: OCP MX v1.0 + ml_dtypes 0.5.4 ground truth

vectors.name: eight
vectors.spec_reference: OCP MX v1.0 + ml_dtypes 0.5.4 ground truth

vectors.name: nan_encoded
vectors.spec_reference: OCP MX v1.0 + ml_dtypes 0.5.4 ground truth

vectors.name: zero_encoded
vectors.spec_reference: OCP MX v1.0 + ml_dtypes 0.5.4 ground truth

vectors.name: overflow
vectors.spec_reference: OCP MX v1.0 + ml_dtypes 0.5.4 ground truth

vectors.name: underflow
vectors.spec_reference: OCP MX v1.0 + ml_dtypes 0.5.4 ground truth

validation.match: documented during cross-validation (OCP MX v1.0 spec compliance)
validation.notes: E8M0 is a scale-only format (no mantissa, no sign). Vectors regenerated using ml_dtypes ground truth, which follows OCP MX v1.0 spec semantics.

cross_vendor_notes: E8M0 is defined by the OCP Microscaling spec v1.0 as the block-scale for MXFP4/MXFP6/MXFP8. Byte 0x00 encodes 2^-127 (smallest scale), byte 0xFF encodes NaN/reserved. Some implementations also use 0x00 for 'block all zeros' fast path. Non-power-of-2 inputs are not natively representable; this pack documents both the ml_dtypes encoding (which uses NaN for out-of-range) and the tt-metal convention (which floors to nearest power-of-2).
